Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reasonable Attorneys Fees
Off Calendar Jul-21-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PETITIONERs PRIYA SANGER and MICHAEL SANGER's Motion For Reasonable Attorneys Fees and costs is granted in the amount of $56,501.18. The award reflects the corrected total sought by Petitioners of $57,139.10, minus $638 sought for time incurred in connection with other actions. All of the remaining fees and costs were incurred by Petitioners in enforcing the underlying judgment, which was originally entered on March 12, 2012, and amended on April 16, 2018. (Code Civ. Proc. 687.070(a), 685.040, 1033.5(a)(10)(A).) The amount of time spent and the hourly rates charged for that time are reasonable in amount and were reasonably and necessarily expended in the underlying proceedings.

With one exception, Respondent's various objections are meritless. (1) Fees incurred by Petitioners in connection with Respondent's unsuccessful appeal to the Ninth Circuit are recoverable. Conservatorship of McQueen (2014) 59 Cal.4th 602 did not hold that appellate fees are not recoverable under Section 685.040, as Respondent contends, but only that "where attorney fees are authorized by statute . . . , fees awarded for expenses incurred on appeal from the trial court judgment are not governed by the procedures of the Enforcement of Judgments Law." (Id. at 608.) In contrast, "postjudgment enforcement costs and fees are recovered under the Enforcement of Judgments Law, specifically sctions 685.040 to 685.095." (Id.) Here, as the Court of Appeal explicitly held, Petitioners' entitlement to fees stems from the Enforcement of Judgments Law, not from a statutory fee provision. Jaffee v. Pacelli (2008) 165 Cal.App.4th 927, is controlling, as that case squarely held that a creditor was entitled to an award of attorney fees and costs pursuant to section 685.040 for the efforts he expended in connection with the debtor's bankruptcy proceedings, including defending against three unsuccessful appeals to the district and one to the Ninth Circuit challenging the bankruptcy court's rulings. (Id. at 932, 938 ["[Creditor's] actions in the bankruptcy proceedings were necessary in order to maintain, preserve, and protect the enforceability of the judgment. [Creditor] successfully blocked [debtor's] efforts to have the debt discharged by the bankruptcy court and [creditor] protected the judgment. [Creditor's] actions in the bankruptcy proceedings are enforcement proceedings pursuant to section 685.040."].)

(2, 3) The costs incurred by Petitioners in connection with the writs of execution and filing the abstract of judgment were reasonably incurred to enforce the judgment. (4) Respondent challenges four entries, totaling $638, relating to fees and costs incurred in other actions. Petitioners concede that one $50 charge was incurred in a different unlawful detainer action and should not be awarded here. The court will not award compensation for the other three entries challenged by Respondent, which relate to another pending action; Petitioners may recover those costs in the event they prevail in that action. (5) Ladas v. California State Auto. Assoc. (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 776, held only that costs of computerized legal research are not recoverable. Based on the court's review of counsel's billing records (Utecht Decl., Ex. A), the three entries Respondent challenges do not reflect Westlaw or LexisNexis charges, but rather attorney time conducting factual or legal research, which is plainly compensable. (6) The challenged FedEx charges reflect reasonable costs of service. (7) Finally, the court rejects Respondent's claim that the fees incurred in connection with the debtor's examination were excessive.

order signed.

No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopts the tentative ruling.

PETITIONERS PRIYA SANGER, MICHAEL SANGER'S MOTION FOR ATTORNEY FEES AND EXPENSES is granted in substantial part. The Sangers are awarded $170,000 as reasonable fees. The Sangers are entitled to recover the bankruptcy attorney's fees per Jaffe v. Pacelli (2008) 165 Cal.App.4th 927. Those fees were incurred as a result of attempting to enforce the judgment and are recoverable. (Jaffe, 165 Cal.App.4th at 938.) The Sangers can recover their legal research fees. When discussing computer legal research costs, Ladas v. CSAA (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 776 stated: "Subdivision (b)(2) precludes recovery of investigative expenses and attorney's fees are not compensable as costs in the absence of an agreement of the parties or statutory authority." (Id. at 776.) To the extent that this language in Ladas suggests that research costs would be recoverable as "attorney's fees" if there were a contractual provision or statute allowing attorney's fees, there is such a provision here, and the costs are recoverable as fees. There is no recovery of the fees for the Adversary Proceeding ($1,006) and postage/ transcript fees ($198.73). The amount of the remaining claimed fees are excessive and reduced. It was unnecessary for the Sangers numerous attorneys to repeatedly "revise" and "redraft" the numerous filings. The fees incurred for motion for fees that was calendared for 2/27/18 were excessive since a large portion of those claimed fees were conceded as improper.

ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Not reported.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopts the tentative ruling.

PETITIONERS PRIYA SANGER and MICHAEL SANGER'S Motion To Amend Judgment is granted. The Sangers are entitled to entry of an amended judgment that reflects that Ms. Ahn owes an additional $27,869.25. CCP 187 provides for the issuance of orders to "carry into effect" a judgment. Section 187 is not limited to adding judgment debtors. Ms. Ahn fails to show that her bankruptcy filing discharged her obligation to pay her share of the joint mortgage. The Bankruptcy Appellate Panel expressly held that such recovery was proper. (Hurwitz Dec., Ex.D., 15:14-23; 17:9-14.) The Sangers are not violating the statute of frauds. Ms. Ahn signed the tenancy in common agreement and agreed to service the mortgage. (Sanger Dec., Ex. A, 4.2A.) The Sangers can seek contribution from Ms. Ahn as a co-tenant to recover proportionate expenses and there is no public policy violation. (Hurwitz Dec., Ex.D., 16:8-14.) There is nothing uncertain about the judgment as the arbitrator already concluded. (Hurwitz Dec., Ex.E. pg. 38.)
